chapters.title: The hope that it is not cancer
chapters.text:
Paul Kalanithi was at the peak of his career as a respectable doctor and led a happy life with his wife, Lucy. His life reached a point where he could provide his family with everything they deserved. However, nothing lasts forever.

One day, Kalanithi started to lose a lot of weight. He had severe back pain and soon suspected it might be cancer. Despite being responsible and seeing a doctor, his symptoms worsened daily. Another thirty pounds evaporated from his body while his back and chest fought for who got it worse. At this point, Kalanithi had almost no doubt about his condition. The only person he did not want to involve was his wife.

However, there was no way to hide the truth from the person he loved the most. Despite his best intentions, secrecy can destroy years of trust in a second; Kalanithi learned this the hard way. Hurt and distraught, Lucy left but returned a week later when the final diagnosis arrived.

Life has unexpected turns, which can be difficult, but facing them helps you achieve peace.

Lonely and tormented, Kalanithi expected nothing but an end to his relationship and the happy life as he knew it. However, when the blurry chest X-ray confirmed his fears of stage IV metastatic lung cancer, he had to reevaluate everything before it was too late. He passed away at the age of thirty-seven. But his experience and thoughts during the period of battle and reconciliation have long since helped other people appreciate every moment just enough to realize they've endured the hardship.

chapters.title: Sometimes, our childhood holds a secret from our adult selves
chapters.text:
As a child, Paul Kalanithi had two struggles: a wild and dangerous nature and his parents. A son of a doctor, he was expected to follow the tradition. As it often happens, Kalanithi desired a different path as a writer. But being an author did not promise financial stability at that time. Another reason he did not fancy becoming a medic was his father. Distant and strict with his family, he did not make the profession attractive to his son. Besides, Kalanithi knew nothing about medicine but knew enough to associate it with an absent father and reheated dinners. Rare hugs and talks were a high price for being a doctor.

Aside from having a compromising experience with his father, a young Paul Kalanithi had trouble adapting to his environment. When he was ten, his family moved to Arizona. An affordable house on a desert plateau was the only way to provide education to the three boys in the family. Kalanithi grew up in wild surroundings, often costing him sleepless nights. After all, not everyone spends their formative years among clusters of black widows, scorpions, and tarantulas. But day after day, anyone would get used to the scary habitat, just as Kalanithi did. One example from his life is remarkable in this sense. As a kid, he stumbled upon a gigantic rattlesnake with his brother. The boys were smart to know that killing it would trigger the entire nest, so they had to be creative. They successfully put the snake into a pillowcase and threw it into the desert. Now listen to this: they even returned to bring back the pillowcase because an angry mom is scarier than any snake!

Kalanithi grew more determined and matured in Arizona, surrounded by age-old nature. Perhaps, it prepared him for his future choice and made it bearable to witness suffering and death.

And while his new hometown had loads of snakes, it had no proper school. Kalanithi's mother, a medical school graduate and a bookworm, insisted that her children read as much as possible. She “vaccinated” Kalanithi with a love for reading so that no other metaphoric snake would scare him. From then on, the boy did not shun sexual references, pain, or rage. The books taught him to open his mind to everything.

Reading broadens our horizons and improves our vocabulary.

chapters.title: In search of meaning and running from reality
chapters.text:
A love for reading can take us to lots of unexpected places. Kalanithi's passion for literature brought him to the Yale School of Medicine. Ironically, the family tradition found a different path to the man's heart. And the way it started was love.

We all become enamored with our first relationship and the people we entrust ourselves to. Kalanithi fell for a sweet girl who worked at a coffee shop to pay her college tuition.

Her name was Abigail, and one day she tossed a “lowbrow” book at her boyfriend, who refused to read anything but “high-cultured crap.” The book was Satan: His Psychotherapy and Cure by the Unfortunate Dr. Kassler, JSPS, telling the story of Lucifer’s search for a therapist and reconciliation with themselves. Surprisingly, it became the first key to Kalanithi's future. And while Abigail found a way to the boy's heart, this book occupied his head. From that moment on, he fell in love with the human mind.

But the sensitive soul of Paul Kalanithi did not lose its sentiment despite his newfound love for “cold” and “rational” science. For him, being in touch with his consciousness was no less important than understanding it. The desire to comprehend the human mind encouraged Kalanithi to work as a prep chef at a nature camp. The wild, mountainous landscape and cold lakes brought Kalanithi a new perspective on life and new friends. Enjoying the pristine nature, he realized that brain neurons connect us with the world in rather poetic ways. At some point, he had a religious sensation watching the sunset that divided the sky into dark and light halves, as if God were separating one from another. For Kalanithi, this experience resembled nothing but finding his meaning in life.

It also made him search for how our mind affects our body, at which point he realized his love for literature could not provide all the answers. Eventually, the road led him to study neuroscience. Without question, the things that Kalanithi participated in during his studies captivated and perturbed him simultaneously. But the most crucial thing was leaving his traumatic memories of his dad's work and finding his own way to medicine.

Life leads us to the destination we don't know we need without our permission.

Did you know? The brain has two parts responsible for language understanding and generation — Broca’s and Wernicke’s areas.

chapters.title: A meeting with death
chapters.text:
There is one thing no one mentions about brain surgeries: it is a fight for the patient's identity. Patients’ tastes, relationships, and preferences can drastically change if the operation goes wrong. They balance life and death for several hours, hoping they return to being the same person they used to be. But living through such an experience never leaves them unchanged. Instead, they contemplate whether their saved life is worth living. Neurosurgeons know this; however, they also know their responsibility is to ensure their patients open their eyes to happiness and health.

The brain oversees all the processes in our body. Careless removal of some of its parts can negatively impact the rest of the patient's life. In other words, their whole personality is in the hands of a neurosurgeon. The surgeon must make tough decisions, even if the patient remains mute or suffers from visual problems for some time after the operation. Patients, too, must be ready for such consequences, and the harmony between the doctor and the sick person is just half the success. After all, patients have a full right to stop fighting, forcing the doctor to watch them fade away.

The first patient Kalanithi had to let go of was an 82-year-old woman. Her health was anything but problematic. Despite her advanced years, her organs were as functional as if she were just 50. The patient's only trouble was constipation. However, after the successful surgery, her condition suddenly declined. Unfortunately, the patient didn't make it after being resuscitated and stabilized. All Kalanithi had left in him was anger and frustration with himself. He couldn't understand how he had failed the woman.

When we face death for the first time, it's impossible not to notice it anymore.

Soon after his first encounter with death, Kalanithi learned to notice when it came. The air suffocates you, and misery beats you in the gut. Not many of his colleagues could handle such a responsibility. But Kalanithi found a way to counterattack his torment and learned to enjoy a successful recovery with euphoria. Instead of surrendering to death, he decided to celebrate his victories.

chapters.title: Wearing your patient’s shoes
chapters.text:
A good surgeon is not the one who stitches the wound's second half while the first one is already healing. It is a slow surgeon, and he's losing to death. Paul Kalanithi picked up this lesson quite soon for a young professional. A good surgeon is fast and precise, and his accuracy earns him the name. They know that one or two millimeters in the wrong direction can destroy the rest of the patient's life.

In one instance in his career, Kalanithi had to operate on an eight-year-old boy. The kid had a tumor on his hypothalamus, puzzling professionals. As a short biology lesson, the hypothalamus manages our sense of satiation. Thanks to this small area deep inside our brain, we can feel thirst and hunger. Not only that, but it also regulates body temperature, mood, and sleep. Unfortunately, the operation did not go well; Kalanithi slightly damaged the boy's hypothalamus. Four years later, he became addicted to gluttony because his hypothalamus wouldn't order him to stop. Kalanithi learned his lesson the hard way.

Time passed, and now he was lying on the same table he used to operate on his patients. The same hospital that gave him a job and meaning was hosting him. He was not bitter because of the lung cancer that caught him off guard. Instead, he was upset to miss the opportunities, just like everyone else. We can all relate to him. Dr. Emma Hayward, his oncologist, had no doubt her patient would practice again soon. However, Kalanithi's case appeared to be more complicated. Having suspected an epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) mutation, he began the research that made him realize he had too little to trade. More specifically, he had no more than two years. Such a course of events led Kalanithi to reevaluate his plans.

Instead of panicking, he kept a positive attitude; for example, he froze his seminal fluid in hopes of having healthy future kids. His career ceased with the diagnosis, so yesterday's neurosurgeon surrendered to his childhood dream of becoming a writer. Having no certainty over his future, Kalanithi managed to stay afloat for as long as possible.

Surrendering to early grief is a choice, even if it seems the only one left.

chapters.title: The final page
chapters.text:
They say that death doesn't happen to you; it happens to your family and friends. Kalanithi understood it clearly when he considered giving the world his last gift: his daughter. When the terminal diagnosis was clear, he and his wife started considering parenthood. On one hand, Kalanithi worried about leaving his wife a single mother without much-needed support. On the other hand, Lucy dreaded seeing her husband pass and having so little time with their child. But Kalanithi was excited to spend the time he had left with a newborn.

Lucy and I both felt that life wasn't about avoiding suffering. ~ Paul Kalanithi, MD

For Paul and Lucy Kalanithi, having a child meant bringing a sense of life to the household that was losing it. Later, Lucy gave birth to their daughter, Elizabeth Acadia, whose first laughs and steps the father witnessed. But as the little girl grew more animated and excited about the world, her daddy couldn't keep up anymore. Cancer doesn't rob us of connections; it drains the energy needed to maintain them. Each day, the person feels like they’re losing their spirit, unable to enjoy things with their dearest ones. Eventually, time stopped moving for Kalanithi.

Despite his exhaustion, he didn't want to leave without a present for his daughter. Writing letters seemed like a good idea, but what kind of person would she become before she could read them? But whoever Elizabeth was supposed to become, she would naturally doubt her path, like all people. So, Kalanithi's last message had to encourage her from beyond. He wanted Elizabeth to know that whenever she felt insecure, one man used to overflow with joy and happiness because of her.

Paul Kalanithi passed away on March 9, 2015. He hadn't finished his final book, so his wife wrote the last pages of it. After all, Kalanithi never stayed alone, even at his writing desk. Elizabeth Acadia was five months old at the time, and despite having so little time with her daddy, they managed to have one happy Christmas together. Friends and family gathered for the last time despite Kalanithi losing mental clarity. Nevertheless, he was prepared, and when he left, his house was an epicenter of love and care.

When the choice to be happy comes, never miss it because happiness is the biggest benefit.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
A life story is always a sensitive thing, almost intimate. We learn to meet death with a whisper, even when we want to scream out of grief. But when an individual's story becomes a public legacy, the gratitude for life becomes too loud to keep to yourself. Paul Kalanithi's story was his gift to his childhood self and the world. Full of detours and lessons, it shows the man’s becoming despite his passing. When a person's life is so bright it overshadows the final breath, it is a story worth sharing. The world was lucky Paul Kalanithi had two passions — neurosurgery and literature.

When Kalanithi found himself inevitably close to the end, he found the courage to do things he had put off for a while. In his writing, he contemplates death and a newfound meaning. Going through all the hurdles associated with cancer, Kalanithi showed us a perspective on life we'll experience someday, but not today. His story encourages us to seek new meanings while we have time to experience life on different fronts. Kalanithi used his time wisely. He and his wife had a beautiful daughter, and yesterday's neurosurgeon became a writer and father. Instead of letting grief enter their house too early, the family filled it with love, warm hugs, and good memories. Only someone who loves life will not give it up easily but won't hold onto it until it hurts. Paul Kalanithi was someone.

Try this


• Read more books by people who wrote about death and meaning in life, such as ancient Greek philosophers.
• Think about the importance of suffering and grief and how you can find value in them.
• Make a bucket list: think of all your goals and wishes.

Read it till the end?
